title: The Colostomy Bag

jokes.text: I'm on trial for the horrible crime of giving a woman who was on line to vote a bottle of water.
jokes.setup: Larry describes his legal troubles to mock jurors
jokes.punchline: The 'horrible crime' is basic human kindness
jokes.explanation: Strong premise that satirizes actual voting laws. Perfect Larry character integration - turning real controversy into self-martyrdom.

jokes.text: Oh! Oh, what a terrible thing. Oh, what a terrible crime. What... What... What... What... What... What... What a terrible crime to give her a bottle of water.
jokes.setup: Larry's sarcastic complaint about the charges
jokes.punchline: Mock juror's over-the-top repetitive agreement builds absurdity
jokes.explanation: Functional escalation but gets repetitive. The stammering 'What' repetition feels forced rather than natural.

jokes.text: I was pretty damn cordial. I asked questions to people. 'Oh, does your son take piano lessons?' 'Does he practice? It's hard to get him to practice. They don't like to practice.'
jokes.setup: Larry claims he was cordial at the party
jokes.punchline: His example of 'cordiality' is mundane small talk about piano practice
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry - thinks boring small talk proves his social skills. Great character specificity in the rambling piano practice tangent.

jokes.text: You know, my father fought in World War II, Iwo Jima. He wasn't one of the ones with the flag. That's fine. Thank you. He was encouraging the flag holders. Yelled encouragement. 'Go, guys! Go! Up that hill!'
jokes.setup: Larry mentions his father's war service, building expectation of heroism
jokes.punchline: His father was just a cheerleader for the actual heroes
jokes.explanation: Brilliant misdirection from iconic heroism to sideline cheerleading. The specific 'Go guys! Up that hill!' is perfectly absurd and quotable.

jokes.text: In the garage, he saw me tossing my keys to the valet. I could be perceived as a big shot. Nobody likes a big shot.
jokes.setup: Larry worries about juror perception
jokes.punchline: His paranoia about a simple key toss making him look like a 'big shot'
jokes.explanation: Pure Larry neurosis - turning mundane action into social anxiety. Sets up the key-tossing running gag perfectly.

jokes.text: I got a great text from Abe. Why don't we leave our professional and personal lives separate, you know? It was Zeckelman, wasn't it? Yeah. Yeah, it was Zeckelman.
jokes.setup: Jeff tries to keep work and personal life separate
jokes.punchline: Immediately caves and admits Larry was right about the name choice
jokes.explanation: Good callback structure showing Jeff's weakness for Larry's influence despite trying to maintain boundaries.

jokes.text: And we like to toss. People like to toss and catch.
jokes.setup: Building on Rodrigo's catching philosophy
jokes.punchline: Larry extends it to a complete theory of human interaction
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective escalation. Larry takes Rodrigo's idea and makes it universal human truth, typical of his grandiose thinking.

jokes.text: Three percent chance something'll happen to me. So... Susie has the power of attorney. She's in charge of all of it. So if something bad happens to me, I don't trust her.
jokes.setup: Jeff explains his medical procedure and legal arrangements
jokes.punchline: Despite giving her power of attorney, he doesn't trust his own wife
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jeff/Susie dynamic setup. The logic gap of giving power to someone you don't trust is peak dysfunctional marriage comedy.

jokes.text: She could do it for any reason. Who knows? Uh, maybe on the way there we get into a fight, which is more than likely.
jokes.setup: Jeff explaining why he doesn't trust Susie with his life
jokes.punchline: Casually admits they'll probably fight on the way to his surgery
jokes.explanation: Great character moment - Jeff so used to fighting with Susie that he treats it as inevitable, even before life-or-death surgery.

jokes.text: I might not even come once a week. I could just call up. I'm unconscious. What the fuck do I know?
jokes.setup: Jeff asks Larry to check on him during recovery
jokes.punchline: Larry's brutally honest assessment that Jeff won't know if he visits anyway
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry logic and callousness. The casual 'What the fuck do I know?' delivery makes this brutally funny and memorable.

jokes.text: Find out what? Oh. Tell her, tell her, tell her. Find out... Find out that I... Was found guilty. In his mock trial.
jokes.setup: Susie asks what she shouldn't find out, creating tension
jokes.punchline: The 'secret' is just Larry's mock trial loss, completely anticlimactic
jokes.explanation: Decent misdirection but the payoff is a bit weak. The stammering sells the awkwardness but doesn't build to a strong enough revelation.

jokes.text: I like you eating a salad, Jeff. Thank you. Very nice. It's delicious and I'm enjoying it. Good. Good for you.
jokes.setup: Tense conversation about the mock trial
jokes.punchline: Larry's random pivot to praising Jeff's salad choice, completely changing subject
jokes.explanation: Typical Larry non-sequitur to avoid tension. Mildly funny but not particularly memorable or well-crafted.

jokes.text: What kind of mutant can't catch a key?
jokes.setup: Jeff suggests key tossing could hurt someone
jokes.punchline: Larry's incredulous response calling non-catchers 'mutants'
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly economical and quotable. 'Mutant' is the perfect word choice - extreme but somehow logical in Larry's worldview.

jokes.text: He lost his sense of smell during COVID, and... sometimes these old cars, they have a musty odor to 'em. He wants your nose.
jokes.setup: Jeff explains why Lewis needs Larry's help car shopping
jokes.punchline: The concept of 'wanting someone's nose' as a service
jokes.explanation: Good setup for the car storyline. 'He wants your nose' is a funny way to phrase the request, making it sound oddly intimate.

jokes.text: My nose isn't so great. Disgusting things I can smell very well. Urine, BO, you know. Urine, I can smell from two blocks away, but you shove a flower up my nose, it does nothing for me.
jokes.setup: Larry admits his nose isn't great
jokes.punchline: His nose only works for disgusting smells, not pleasant ones
jokes.explanation: Great character specificity - Larry's nose being calibrated only for unpleasant things fits his pessimistic worldview perfectly.

jokes.text: You need clearance. You need clearance before you talk to him. Otherwise, you know, my God. Could you get me clearance? I can get you Conan clearance.
jokes.setup: Lewis wants to approach Conan O'Brien
jokes.punchline: The concept of needing 'clearance' to talk to a celebrity neighbor
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist concept. 'Conan clearance' sounds official and ridiculous simultaneously, setting up a major running gag.

jokes.text: What do you mean it's not any good? A head nod, you're supposed to go like this. Huh. Huh. No, no, you didn't do that. You went like this. Like that.
jokes.setup: Larry criticizes Lewis's head nod technique
jokes.punchline: Larry demonstrates proper vs improper head nodding with specific gestures
jokes.explanation: Classic Larry micro-managing of social interactions. The physical demonstration would be visually funny, typical of his obsession with proper etiquette.

jokes.text: And you use your eyes too? You use your eyes, yeah. There you go. There you go. How come now you tell me?
jokes.setup: Larry continues head nod instruction
jokes.punchline: Lewis's exasperation that Larry didn't teach him proper technique earlier
jokes.explanation: Decent follow-up showing Lewis buying into Larry's nonsense. The exasperation feels genuine but not particularly memorable.

jokes.text: There's a smell. It smells like tobacco. Yeah, like somebody smoked a lot of Chesterfields in this car.
jokes.setup: Larry is supposed to evaluate the car's smell
jokes.punchline: His very specific identification of 'Chesterfields' cigarettes
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'Chesterfields' adds believability and humor. Sets up the smell discount negotiation perfectly.

jokes.text: You know what I smell? I smell a scam. You come here. 'Oh, he can't smell...' 'but I'm gonna tell him, Oh, yeah, smells like cigarettes. Why don't you knock off 3,000?'
jokes.setup: The car seller gets suspicious of Larry and Lewis
jokes.punchline: Accuses them of running an elaborate smell-based con game
jokes.explanation: Great paranoid escalation. The idea of a 'smell scam' is absurd but logical enough to be believable, creating perfect conflict.

jokes.text: Hey, things could be worse. You could have a colostomy bag.
jokes.setup: Lewis complains about his bad day
jokes.punchline: Larry's attempt at perspective with an oddly specific medical reference
jokes.explanation: Classic Larry inappropriate comfort. The randomness of 'colostomy bag' as a perspective check is perfectly awkward and foreshadows later plot.

jokes.text: Do you know if DiCarlo has a colostomy bag? What? What are you smoking? What kind of question is that?
jokes.setup: Larry's random colostomy comment leads to a question
jokes.punchline: Lewis's shocked reaction to Larry asking about someone's medical equipment
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the awkwardness. Larry's casual medical inquiry about an acquaintance is perfectly inappropriate and in character.

jokes.text: Yeah, because I made a bad joke, and I think I... I think I struck a nerve.
jokes.setup: Larry explains why he's asking about DiCarlo
jokes.punchline: His dawning realization that he may have insulted someone with a colostomy bag
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry self-awareness arriving too late. The phrase 'struck a nerve' is ironically appropriate for medical equipment humor.

jokes.text: I saw you look askance, though, when you saw it. Okay, I thought it was kind of a big shot move.
jokes.setup: Larry confronts the valet about his key toss disapproval
jokes.punchline: Larry using 'askance' (a fancy word) while Victor gives a straightforward response
jokes.explanation: Larry's pretentious vocabulary ('askance') contrasted with the simple accusation is typical of his character. Good callback development.

jokes.text: There are two people in the valet world, sir. Handers and tossers. And you're a tosser.
jokes.setup: Victor explains his philosophy about key exchange
jokes.punchline: Creates a binary classification system with Larry as a 'tosser'
jokes.explanation: 'Handers and tossers' is brilliantly quotable taxonomy. The formal delivery makes it funnier, and 'you're a tosser' has double meaning.

jokes.text: I gotta go sign some papers so I can kill my friend whenever I want to.
jokes.setup: Larry explains where he's going to Victor
jokes.punchline: Describes medical power of attorney as license to kill
jokes.explanation: Incredibly dark and economical. Turning power of attorney into assassination permit is peak Larry logic and perfectly quotable.

jokes.text: Oh! You're a dead man. Pull that plug! Pull it! Pull that plug, nurse!
jokes.setup: They've just signed medical power of attorney papers
jokes.punchline: Larry immediately jokes about using his new power to kill Jeff
jokes.explanation: Good immediate callback showing Larry acting on his earlier threat. The enthusiastic 'Pull it!' adds to the dark humor.

jokes.text: Could you believe he didn't give me that pen? I was shocked. He resisted the 'I like it' gambit.
jokes.setup: Larry complained about the lawyer's pen multiple times
jokes.punchline: Reveals the 'I like it' was a calculated strategy to get the pen
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry - turns complimenting into a 'gambit.' The term 'I like it gambit' suggests this is a standard Larry manipulation technique.

jokes.text: He's got a bag. He's got the bag. Unbelievable. I... I don't know. What am I gonna do now?
jokes.setup: Larry learns DiCarlo actually has a colostomy bag
jokes.punchline: Larry's horror at realizing his joke was about someone's real medical condition
jokes.explanation: Good payoff to the colostomy setup. Larry's panic is believable and his repetitive 'the bag' shows his discomfort with medical terminology.

jokes.text: What can I do for this guy? Can I buy him a new bag? Like a really expensive colostomy... Like a Louis Vuitton colostomy bag? Do they sell those? Do they make those?
jokes.setup: Larry wants to make amends for his colostomy bag joke
jokes.punchline: Suggests buying a luxury designer colostomy bag as an apology gift
jokes.explanation: 'Louis Vuitton colostomy bag' is brilliantly absurd and quotable. Perfect Larry logic - throwing money at awkward social situations with luxury goods.

jokes.text: You're Frick and Frack, attached at the hip. Frick and Frack, the ice skating team... From the '30s. A lot of people don't know that. Swedish. I think they're Swiss.
jokes.setup: Susie calls Larry and Jeff 'Frick and Frack'
jokes.punchline: Larry provides unnecessary historical context and gets their nationality wrong
jokes.explanation: Classic Larry pedantry with incorrect information. The Swedish/Swiss confusion adds to his know-it-all character who gets details wrong.

jokes.text: What, is there a difference? So, you need him? He doesn't know the difference between Sweden and Switzerland.
jokes.setup: Larry confused Sweden and Switzerland
jokes.punchline: Jeff's incredulous response that there's obviously a difference, Susie's attack on Larry's intelligence
jokes.explanation: Solid follow-up showing the dynamic between all three characters. Jeff's defense of basic geography is reasonable but not particularly funny.

jokes.text: What are you looking for? I'm looking for the cheese. Oh, it's gone. What? I ate it. You ate the Vonderdonk? Yeah.
jokes.setup: Larry returns to get his stored cheese
jokes.punchline: Susie casually admits to eating his expensive cheese
jokes.explanation: Good setup for the cheese conflict. The casual delivery of cheese theft contrasts with Larry's anticipated outrage.

jokes.text: It was in my refrigerator, and I believe possession is nine-tenths of the law. What's the other tenth? Fucking over your friends and eating their cheese?
jokes.setup: Susie justifies eating the cheese with legal principle
jokes.punchline: Larry's sarcastic completion of the legal maxim with cheese betrayal
jokes.explanation: Brilliant legal humor. Larry's completion of 'nine-tenths of the law' with friendship betrayal is perfectly crafted and quotable.

jokes.text: If I took a nap on your couch, could you go through my pockets and roll me over like a drunk?
jokes.setup: Larry argues against Susie's possession logic
jokes.punchline: Analogizes cheese theft to mugging a sleeping person
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation with vivid imagery. 'Roll me over like a drunk' is specific and funny, making the cheese theft seem criminal.

jokes.text: It's nine-tenths of the fucking law!
jokes.setup: Larry storms out with Susie's pomegranate juice
jokes.punchline: Uses her own legal argument to justify stealing her juice
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback structure. Larry turning Susie's logic against her while stealing her juice is satisfying comedic justice.

jokes.text: I specifically called him up and asked if he could get me clearance. He didn't call me, so technically, this is a uncleared conversation. It's like an ambush.
jokes.setup: Larry approaches Conan without permission
jokes.punchline: Treats casual conversation like military operation requiring clearance
jokes.explanation: 'Uncleared conversation' and 'ambush' make normal social interaction sound like espionage. Perfect escalation of the clearance absurdity.

jokes.text: 'Oh, I assume this boat has plenty of life preservers for everyone. Oh, no, it's the Titanic, it doesn't.'
jokes.setup: Conan explains why you don't assume things
jokes.punchline: Uses the Titanic as an example of dangerous assumptions
jokes.explanation: Good analogy escalating from clearance to maritime disaster. Shows Conan's comedic timing even in unscripted conversation.

jokes.text: Sure. Yeah, I'll think about it. Ah. You're not gonna think about it. That's just something people say... When they wanna say no, and they let the person down a little easier.
jokes.setup: Conan says he'll think about giving Larry clearance
jokes.punchline: Larry immediately calls out the polite lie, Conan agrees
jokes.explanation: Excellent meta-commentary on social politeness. Both characters acknowledge the lie immediately, creating honest awkwardness.

jokes.text: But you won't spend one second thinking about it. Yeah, no. The minute I'm gone, I'll never think about it again.
jokes.setup: Larry continues analyzing Conan's polite rejection
jokes.punchline: Conan's brutally honest confirmation of Larry's assessment
jokes.explanation: Great follow-up with Conan's complete honesty. The casual admission of immediate forgetting is perfectly delivered.

jokes.text: Can you stereotype a dog? Yes, you can stereotype a dog. 'Oh, that's a golden. He must be a really friendly dog.' 'Oh, there's a Dalmatian. He must live in a firehouse. He goes to fires.'
jokes.setup: Larry questions whether dogs can be stereotyped
jokes.punchline: Conan's examples of dog breed stereotypes, especially Dalmatians going to fires
jokes.explanation: Good observational humor about breed expectations. 'He goes to fires' is a funny way to describe the Dalmatian stereotype.

jokes.text: I read an article about somebody approaching Fatty Arbuckle without clearance, and Fatty Arbuckle just broke a beer bottle over his head.
jokes.setup: Larry explains the historical precedent for celebrity clearance
jokes.punchline: Claims Fatty Arbuckle violently attacked someone for approaching without permission
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist escalation. Connecting modern celebrity culture to 1920s silent film stars with violence is peak Larry historical revisionism.

jokes.text: What, did you just come from Margaret Dumont's house?
jokes.setup: Larry makes another old Hollywood reference
jokes.punchline: Conan's sarcastic response referencing an even older performer
jokes.explanation: Sharp comeback from Conan. Margaret Dumont reference (Marx Brothers straight woman) tops Larry's obscure reference game.

jokes.text: You say a lot of stupid things, Larry. Not all jokes work. Not a joke to me.
jokes.setup: Larry apologizes for his bad joke
jokes.punchline: DiCarlo's blunt assessment and revelation that it wasn't funny to him
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing DiCarlo's hurt. The dismissal of Larry's comedy attempts is harsh but justified given the context.

jokes.text: Yeah, well, we all got a lot of baggage.
jokes.setup: Larry and DiCarlo discussing their respective problems
jokes.punchline: DiCarlo's double meaning reference to both emotional and literal 'baggage'
jokes.explanation: Brilliant double entendre. 'Baggage' works both metaphorically and literally for someone with a colostomy bag. Perfect economy and character integration.

jokes.text: Look at the front. Very presidential. Look at that. Look at that grill. This is a White man car right here, I'll tell you that much. Who'd you get it from, Mr. Peanut? The motherfucker with the monocle and a top hat?
jokes.setup: Leon examining Larry's new vintage Mercedes
jokes.punchline: Leon's assessment of the car's demographic and absurd seller description
jokes.explanation: Perfect Leon character voice. 'Mr. Peanut with the monocle' is brilliantly specific and absurd imagery for a white luxury car owner.

jokes.text: Hey, did Marilyn Monroe fuck JFK in a car like this?
jokes.setup: Leon continues examining the vintage car
jokes.punchline: Random historical conspiracy theory sexual question
jokes.explanation: Typical Leon non-sequitur connecting historical figures to current situation. The casual delivery of scandalous historical speculation is funny.

jokes.text: The guy actually has a colostomy bag, and I felt terrible. I felt so guilty and awful, I had to buy the car.
jokes.setup: Lewis demands to know why Larry bought the car
jokes.punchline: Larry's guilt-driven purchase explanation
jokes.explanation: Good payoff to the colostomy storyline. Larry's guilt-driven expensive purchase is believable character behavior.

jokes.text: Why didn't I get clearance for Conan? Where was my Conan clearance? You left me hanging there like an idiot.
jokes.setup: Lewis wants the car back from Larry
jokes.punchline: Larry deflects by blaming Lewis for the Conan clearance failure
jokes.explanation: Good callback integration. Larry's deflection technique of bringing up unrelated grievances is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: There's a smell clinic, but it's in Switzerland. I'm not sure if I'm gonna go.
jokes.setup: Lewis claims he might get surgery to restore his smell
jokes.punchline: Mentions a specialized Swiss smell clinic
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist detail. Switzerland as the location for specialty medical tourism feels believable but ridiculous.

jokes.text: Did you babe me? I babed you. Don't babe me. Do not, under any circumstances, babe me ever, ever.
jokes.setup: Lewis accidentally calls Larry 'babe'
jokes.punchline: Larry's intense reaction to the casual endearment
jokes.explanation: Good masculine discomfort comedy. Larry's horror at casual intimacy and the verb 'babed' is funny character-specific reaction.

jokes.text: Toss? Yeah, come on. I don't wanna be a big shot. You're not a big shot. Come on, toss it. All right. Awesome. There we go. Fun, right? It's fun! Of course! He's a tosser.
jokes.setup: Victor offers to let Larry toss the keys
jokes.punchline: Their mutual enjoyment of key tossing, Victor calling Larry a 'tosser' approvingly
jokes.explanation: Good callback resolution. The mutual enjoyment validates Larry's earlier position, and 'tosser' becomes positive instead of accusatory.

jokes.text: Um, the word 'repugnant' keeps coming up. Here's one that says you're 'surly,' 'petty.'
jokes.setup: Lawyer reading jury feedback about Larry
jokes.punchline: The harsh but accurate descriptors from the mock jury
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy - the jury's assessment perfectly captures Larry's personality flaws.

jokes.text: First of all, this isn't 12 Angry Men. There's one angry man. He's sitting across from me.
jokes.setup: Larry compares his situation to 12 Angry Men
jokes.punchline: Lawyer's correction that Larry is the one angry man
jokes.explanation: Excellent reversal of Larry's movie reference. The lawyer's observation that Larry is the angry one is perfectly delivered and quotable.

jokes.text: Hey. Yeah? Catch. Ziggy Zeckelman! Not too late!
jokes.setup: Jeff tosses something to Larry
jokes.punchline: Celebrates with the baby name from earlier episode
jokes.explanation: Decent callback combining the baby name and catching themes. 'Not too late' adds urgency but the connection feels slightly forced.

jokes.text: Hey, you tossed, you lost, buddy.
jokes.setup: Larry's car won't start after key tossing celebration
jokes.punchline: Victor's play on 'you snooze, you lose' with tossing theme
jokes.explanation: Brilliant wordplay turning the tossing motif into comeuppance. Perfect economy and timing for Larry's car trouble.

jokes.text: If I don't get to Jeff's house, she's gonna see those papers, and she's gonna kill us. You don't know what this woman's capable of. All right, I don't know who these people are. I'll get you the keys.
jokes.setup: Larry desperately needs to borrow Conan's car
jokes.punchline: Conan's bewildered agreement despite not understanding the situation
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of Larry's panic. Conan's confused but helpful response shows his character while advancing the crisis.

jokes.text: Can't I get an emergency clearance exemption? There's no such thing! You can... You can make... You gotta go through the levels!
jokes.setup: Larry's car emergency and need for help
jokes.punchline: Even in an emergency, the clearance bureaucracy must be maintained
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation of the clearance absurdity - even genuine emergencies can't override the protocol.

jokes.text: You got something on your glasses. Oh. Oh, thank you! That's so nice of you. Thank you. They're in my pocket. Ah! My eye! Why'd you toss? Who told you to toss?
jokes.setup: Larry tries to help clean Conan's glasses
jokes.punchline: Accidentally injures Conan by tossing the glasses, then blames him for the toss
jokes.explanation: Perfect physical comedy payoff. Larry's automatic tossing instinct causing injury, then victim-blaming Conan, is peak Larry behavior.

jokes.text: He acted like he had a bag. How does somebody act like they have a colostomy bag? He had this hangdog 'I shit in a bag' look about him, and he took me to the cleaners.
jokes.setup: Larry explains how DiCarlo deceived him
jokes.punchline: Larry's crude but specific description of the deceptive expression
jokes.explanation: 'Hangdog I shit in a bag look' is crudely specific and perfectly Larry. The revelation that he was conned adds insult to injury.

jokes.text: What, am I supposed to just give up and crawl under a rock? Yes! Give up. There's no hope.
jokes.setup: Lewis defends his romantic optimism at his age
jokes.punchline: Larry's brutally honest assessment of Lewis's dating prospects
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry pessimism delivered with deadpan cruelty. 'Give up. There's no hope' is both funny and harsh friendship advice.

jokes.text: What? Oh, God! It fucking stinks in here. What are you talking about? Hey, where you going? Oh! Hey, what's wrong? Oh, man! Hey! Come on back!
jokes.setup: Lewis picks up his date in the smelly car
jokes.punchline: The woman immediately flees from the tobacco smell Larry identified
jokes.explanation: Perfect payoff to the smell storyline. Larry's nose was right, and Lewis's romantic hopes are dashed by tobacco odor. Great dramatic irony.

tmdb_overview: When Richard enlists Larry's help to buy a vintage car, his careless behavior leads to an unwanted outcome. Jeff secretly schemes to give Larry power of attorney.
